# Fork of Original QuantStats by Ran Aroussi

This is a forked version of the original QuantStats library by Ran Aroussi. The original library can be found at <https://github.com/ranaroussi/quantstats>

This forked version was created because it seems that the original library is no longer being maintained. The original library has a number of issues and pull requests that have been open for a long time and have not been addressed. This forked version aims to address some of these issues and pull requests.

This forked version is created and maintained by the Lumiwealth team. We are a team of data scientists and software engineers who are passionate about quantitative finance and algorithmic trading. We use QuantStats in our daily work with the Lumibot library and we want to make sure that QuantStats is a reliable and well-maintained library.

If you're interested in learning how to make your own trading algorithms, check out our Lumibot library at <https://github.com/Lumiwealth/lumibot> and check out our courses at <https://lumiwealth.com>

# QuantStats: Portfolio analytics for quants

**QuantStats** Python library that performs portfolio profiling, allowing quants and portfolio managers to understand their performance better by providing them with in-depth analytics and risk metrics.

[Changelog »](./CHANGELOG.rst)

## QuantStats is comprised of 3 main modules:

1. `quantstats.stats` - for calculating various performance metrics, like Sharpe ratio, Win rate, Volatility, etc.
2. `quantstats.plots` - for visualizing performance, drawdowns, rolling statistics, monthly returns, etc.
3. `quantstats.reports` - for generating metrics reports, batch plotting, and creating tear sheets that can be saved as an HTML file.

Here's an example of a simple tear sheet analyzing a strategy:

# Quick Start

Install QuantStats Lumi using pip:

```bash
$ pip install quantstats-lumi
```

```python
%matplotlib inline
import quantstats_lumi as qs

# extend pandas functionality with metrics, etc.
qs.extend_pandas()

# fetch the daily returns for a stock
stock = qs.utils.download_returns('META')

# show sharpe ratio
qs.stats.sharpe(stock)

# or using extend_pandas() :)
stock.sharpe()
```

Output:

```text
0.8135304438803402
```

## Visualize stock performance

```python
qs.plots.snapshot(stock, title='Facebook Performance', show=True)

# can also be called via:
# stock.plot_snapshot(title='Facebook Performance', show=True)
```

Output:

```{image} https://github.com/ranaroussi/quantstats/blob/main/docs/snapshot.jpg?raw=true
:alt: Snapshot plot
```

## Creating a report

You can create 7 different report tearsheets:

1. `qs.reports.metrics(mode='basic|full", ...)` - shows basic/full metrics
2. `qs.reports.plots(mode='basic|full", ...)` - shows basic/full plots
3. `qs.reports.basic(...)` - shows basic metrics and plots
4. `qs.reports.full(...)` - shows full metrics and plots
5. `qs.reports.html(...)` - generates a complete report as html

Let' create an html tearsheet

```python
(benchmark can be a pandas Series or ticker)
qs.reports.html(stock, "SPY")
```

Output will generate something like this:

```{image} https://github.com/ranaroussi/quantstats/blob/main/docs/report.jpg?raw=true
:alt: HTML tearsheet
```

([view original html file](https://rawcdn.githack.com/ranaroussi/quantstats/main/docs/tearsheet.html))

**\*\*\* Full documenttion coming soon \*\*\***

In the meantime, you can get insights as to optional parameters for each method, by using Python's `help` method:

```python
help(qs.stats.conditional_value_at_risk)
```

```text
Help on function conditional_value_at_risk in module quantstats.stats:

conditional_value_at_risk(returns, sigma=1, confidence=0.99)
    calculats the conditional daily value-at-risk (aka expected shortfall)
    quantifies the amount of tail risk an investment
```

### Installation

Install using `pip`:

```bash
$ pip install quantstats --upgrade --no-cache-dir
```

Install using `conda`:

```bash
$ conda install -c ranaroussi quantstats
```

### Requirements

- [Python](https://www.python.org) >= 3.5+
- [pandas](https://github.com/pydata/pandas) (tested to work with >=0.24.0)
- [numpy](http://www.numpy.org) >= 1.15.0
- [scipy](https://www.scipy.org) >= 1.2.0
- [matplotlib](https://matplotlib.org) >= 3.0.0
- [seaborn](https://seaborn.pydata.org) >= 0.9.0
- [tabulate](https://bitbucket.org/astanin/python-tabulate) >= 0.8.0
- [yfinance](https://github.com/ranaroussi/yfinance) >= 0.1.38
- [plotly](https://plot.ly/) >= 3.4.1 (optional, for using `plots.to_plotly()`)

### Questions?

This is a new library... If you find a bug, please
[open an issue](https://github.com/ranaroussi/quantstats/issues)
in this repository.

If you'd like to contribute, a great place to look is the
[issues marked with help-wanted](https://github.com/ranaroussi/quantstats/issues?q=is%3Aopen+is%3Aissue+label%3A%22help+wanted%22).

### Known Issues

For some reason, I couldn't find a way to tell seaborn not to return the
monthly returns heatmap when instructed to save - so even if you save the plot (by passing `savefig={...}`) it will still show the plot.

### Legal Stuff

**QuantStats** is distributed under the **Apache Software License**. See the [LICENSE.txt](./LICENSE.txt) file in the release for details.

### P.S.

Please drop me a note with any feedback you have.

**Ran Aroussi**

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils.rebase
documentation:
Rebase all series to a given intial base.
This makes comparing/plotting different series together easier.
Args:
    * prices: Expects a price series/dataframe
    * base (number): starting value for all series.

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils.group_returns
documentation:
Summarize returns
group_returns(df, df.index.year)
group_returns(df, [df.index.year, df.index.month])

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils.aggregate_returns
documentation: Aggregates returns based on date periods

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils.to_excess_returns
documentation:
Calculates excess returns by subtracting
risk-free returns from total returns

Args:
    * returns (Series, DataFrame): Returns
    * rf (float, Series, DataFrame): Risk-Free rate(s)
    * nperiods (int): Optional. If provided, will convert rf to different
        frequency using deannualize
Returns:
    * excess_returns (Series, DataFrame): Returns - rf

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils._prepare_prices
documentation: Converts return data into prices + cleanup

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils._prepare_returns
documentation: Converts price data into returns + cleanup

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils._prepare_benchmark
documentation:
Fetch benchmark if ticker is provided, and pass through
_prepare_returns()

period can be options or (expected) _pd.DatetimeIndex range

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils._round_to_closest
documentation: Round to closest resolution

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils._file_stream
documentation: Returns a file stream

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils._in_notebook
documentation: Identify enviroment (notebook, terminal, etc)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils._count_consecutive
documentation: Counts consecutive data (like cumsum() with reset on zeroes)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils._score_str
documentation: Returns + sign for positive values (used in plots)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils.make_index
documentation:
Makes an index out of the given tickers and weights.
Optionally you can pass a dataframe with the returns.
If returns is not given it try to download them with yfinance

Args:
    * ticker_weights (Dict): A python dict with tickers as keys
        and weights as values
    * rebalance: Pandas resample interval or None for never
    * period: time period of the returns to be downloaded
    * returns (Series, DataFrame): Optional. Returns If provided,
        it will fist check if returns for the given ticker are in
        this dataframe, if not it will try to download them with
        yfinance
Returns:
    * index_returns (Series, DataFrame): Returns for the index

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.utils.make_portfolio
documentation: Calculates compounded value of portfolio

documentation: Dirty method for flattening multi-index dataframe

documentation: match dates of returns and benchmark

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.reports.html
documentation:
Generates a full HTML tearsheet with performance metrics and plots

Parameters
----------
returns : pd.Series, pd.DataFrame
    Strategy returns
benchmark : pd.Series, optional
    Benchmark returns
rf : float, optional
    Risk-free rate, default is 0
grayscale : bool, optional
    Plot in grayscale, default is False
title : str, optional
    Title of the HTML report, default is "Strategy Tearsheet"
output : str, optional
    Output file path
compounded : bool, optional
    Whether to use compounded returns, default is True
periods_per_year : int, optional
    Trading periods per year, default is 365
download_filename : str, optional
    Download filename, default is "tearsheet.html"
figfmt : str, optional
    Figure format, default is "svg"
template_path : str, optional
    Custom template path
match_dates : bool, optional
    Match dates of returns and benchmark, default is True
parameters : dict, optional
    Strategy parameters

Returns
-------
None

documentation: calculates and plots full performance metrics

documentation: calculates and plots basic performance metrics

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.autocorr_penalty
documentation: Metric to account for auto correlation

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.sharpe
documentation:
Calculates the sharpe ratio of access returns

If rf is non-zero, you must specify periods.
In this case, rf is assumed to be expressed in yearly (annualized) terms

Args:
    * returns (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * rf (float): Risk-free rate expressed as a yearly (annualized) return
    * periods (int): Freq. of returns
    * annualize: return annualize sharpe?
    * smart: return smart sharpe ratio

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.smart_sharpe
documentation:
Calculates the smart sharpe ratio
Args:
    * returns (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * rf (float): Risk-free rate expressed as a yearly (annualized) return
    * periods (int): Freq. of returns
    * annualize: return annualize sharpe?

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.rolling_sharpe
documentation:
Calculates the rolling sharpe ratio
Args:
    * returns (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * rf (float): Risk-free rate expressed as a yearly (annualized) return
    * rolling_period (int): Rolling period
    * annualize: return annualize sharpe?
    * periods_per_year: periods per year

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.sortino
documentation:
Calculates the sortino ratio of access returns

If rf is non-zero, you must specify periods.
In this case, rf is assumed to be expressed in yearly (annualized) terms

Calculation is based on this paper by Red Rock Capital
http://www.redrockcapital.com/Sortino__A__Sharper__Ratio_Red_Rock_Capital.pdf

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.smart_sortino
documentation:
Calculates the smart sortino ratio
Args:
    * returns (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * rf (float): Risk-free rate expressed as a yearly (annualized) return
    * periods (int): Freq. of returns
    * annualize: return annualize sharpe?

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.rolling_sortino
documentation:
Calculates the rolling sortino ratio
Args:
    * returns (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * rf (float): Risk-free rate expressed as a yearly (annualized) return
    * rolling_period (int): Rolling period
    * annualize: return annualize sharpe?
    * periods_per_year: periods per year

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.adjusted_sortino
documentation:
Jack Schwager's version of the Sortino ratio allows for
direct comparisons to the Sharpe. See here for more info:
https://archive.is/wip/2rwFW

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.probabilistic_ratio
documentation:
Calculates the probabilistic sharpe ratio
Args:
    * series (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * rf (float): Risk-free rate expressed as a yearly (annualized) return
    * periods (int): Freq. of returns
    * annualize: return annualize sharpe?
    * smart: return smart sharpe ratio

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.probabilistic_sharpe_ratio
documentation:
Calculates the probabilistic sharpe ratio
Args:
    * series (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * rf (float): Risk-free rate expressed as a yearly (annualized) return
    * periods (int): Freq. of returns
    * annualize: return annualize sharpe?
    * smart: return smart sharpe ratio

documentation:
Calculates the probabilistic sortino ratio
Args:
    * series (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * rf (float): Risk-free rate expressed as a yearly (annualized) return
    * periods (int): Freq. of returns
    * annualize: return annualize sharpe?
    * smart: return smart sharpe ratio

documentation:
Calculates the probabilistic adjusted sortino ratio

Args:

    * rf (float): Risk-free rate expressed as a yearly (annualized) return
    * periods (int): Freq. of returns

documentation:
Calculates the Treynor ratio

Args:
    * returns (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * benchmatk (String, Series, DataFrame): Benchmark to compare beta to
    * periods (int): Freq. of returns

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.omega
documentation:
Determines the Omega ratio of a strategy.
See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omega_ratio for more details.

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.gain_to_pain_ratio
documentation:
Jack Schwager's GPR. See here for more info:
https://archive.is/wip/2rwFW

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.cagr
documentation:
Calculates the communicative annualized growth return
(CAGR%) of access returns

If rf is non-zero, you must specify periods.
In this case, rf is assumed to be expressed in yearly (annualized) terms

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.rar
documentation:
Calculates the risk-adjusted return of access returns
(CAGR / exposure. takes time into account.)

If rf is non-zero, you must specify periods.
In this case, rf is assumed to be expressed in yearly (annualized) terms

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.skew
documentation:
Calculates returns' skewness
(the degree of asymmetry of a distribution around its mean)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.kurtosis
documentation:
Calculates returns' kurtosis
(the degree to which a distribution peak compared to a normal distribution)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.calmar
documentation: Calculates the calmar ratio (CAGR% / MaxDD%)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.ulcer_index
documentation: Calculates the ulcer index score (downside risk measurment)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.ulcer_performance_index
documentation:
Calculates the ulcer index score
(downside risk measurment)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.upi
documentation: Shorthand for ulcer_performance_index()

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.serenity_index
documentation:
Calculates the serenity index score
(https://www.keyquant.com/Download/GetFile?Filename=%5CPublications%5CKeyQuant_WhitePaper_APT_Part1.pdf)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.risk_of_ruin
documentation:
Calculates the risk of ruin
(the likelihood of losing all one's investment capital)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.ror
documentation: Shorthand for risk_of_ruin()

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.value_at_risk
documentation:
Calculats the daily value-at-risk
(variance-covariance calculation with confidence n)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.var
documentation: Shorthand for value_at_risk()

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.conditional_value_at_risk
documentation:
Calculats the conditional daily value-at-risk (aka expected shortfall)
quantifies the amount of tail risk an investment

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.cvar
documentation: Shorthand for conditional_value_at_risk()

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.expected_shortfall
documentation: Shorthand for conditional_value_at_risk()

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.tail_ratio
documentation:
Measures the ratio between the right
(95%) and left tail (5%).

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.payoff_ratio
documentation: Measures the payoff ratio (average win/average loss)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.win_loss_ratio
documentation: Shorthand for payoff_ratio()

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.profit_ratio
documentation: Measures the profit ratio (win ratio / loss ratio)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.profit_factor
documentation: Measures the profit ratio (wins/loss)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.cpc_index
documentation:
Measures the cpc ratio
(profit factor * win % * win loss ratio)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.common_sense_ratio
documentation: Measures the common sense ratio (profit factor * tail ratio)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.outlier_win_ratio
documentation:
Calculates the outlier winners ratio
99th percentile of returns / mean positive return

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.outlier_loss_ratio
documentation:
Calculates the outlier losers ratio
1st percentile of returns / mean negative return

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.recovery_factor
documentation: Measures how fast the strategy recovers from drawdowns

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.risk_return_ratio
documentation:
Calculates the return / risk ratio
(sharpe ratio without factoring in the risk-free rate)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.max_drawdown
documentation: Calculates the maximum drawdown

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.to_drawdown_series
documentation: Convert returns series to drawdown series

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.drawdown_details
documentation:
Calculates drawdown details, including start/end/valley dates,
duration, max drawdown and max dd for 99% of the dd period
for every drawdown period

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.kelly_criterion
documentation:
Calculates the recommended maximum amount of capital that
should be allocated to the given strategy, based on the
Kelly Criterion (http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kelly_criterion)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.benchmark_correlation
documentation: Calculates the correlation to the benchmark
Calculate the correlation to the benchmark

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.r_squared
documentation: Measures the straight line fit of the equity curve
==== VS. BENCHMARK ====

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.r2
documentation: Shorthand for r_squared()

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.information_ratio
documentation:
Calculates the information ratio
(basically the risk return ratio of the net profits)

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.greeks
documentation: Calculates alpha and beta of the portfolio

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.rolling_greeks
documentation: Calculates rolling alpha and beta of the portfolio

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.compare
documentation:
Compare returns to benchmark on a
day/week/month/quarter/year basis

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.monthly_returns
documentation: Calculates monthly returns

functions.name: quantstats_lumi.stats.romad
documentation:
Calculates the romad (return/cagr over max drawdown) of a strategy
Args:
    * returns (Series, DataFrame): Input return series
    * periods (int): Freq. of returns
    * annualize: return annualize sharpe?
    * smart: return smart sharpe ratio
Calculate the romad (return/cagr over max drawdown) of a strategy
